topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the hairline fracture on the casing with a fingertip, a slow, deliberate movement that seemed to anchor her against the swirling uncertainty in the room. The faint scent of ozone and soldering flux hung heavy in the air. She leaned back, the cheap plastic chair groaning in protest, and stared at the schematic spread across the workbench. Then, her gaze flicked across the room, landing on a matching, half-finished prototype resting on a nearby shelf—an exact replica of her own current endeavor. A cold knot tightened in her stomach. She swallowed hard, the sound loud in the silence. Who else knew about this? A tremor ran up her arm, making her grip the stylus so tightly her knuckles turned white. She needed to confirm it, to see if the impossible was real.

[story 2]

Mark kept wiping his brow with the back of his hand, his movements jerky and uncoordinated. The glow of the monitor reflected in his tired eyes, illuminating the identical lines of code blinking on screen. He felt a sudden, dizzying lurch in his chest, a feeling like being off balance on a high wire. He pushed himself up from the stool, his legs feeling unsteady. He walked to the adjacent workstation, his boots clicking softly on the linoleum floor. There, sitting beside a pristine, untouched box of components, was a project identical to his own. He stopped dead, his breath catching in his throat. He didn't dare touch it, just stood there, the air seeming to thin around him. A deep sigh escaped him, ragged and shaky.

[story 3]

For weeks, Anya had been wrestling with the final calibration sequence, the frustration manifesting as a constant, low-grade tension in her jaw. She reread the initial design notes, searching for a misplaced variable, anything to explain the uncanny feeling that she wasn't alone in this meticulous, lonely endeavor. When she finally looked up, her eyes snagged on the workbench across the room. There, bathed in the harsh overhead light, sat a device—a flawless mirror of the one she had been painstakingly assembling. Her hands, which had been steady moments before, began to tremble almost imperceptibly. She took a slow, shallow breath, the movement seeming too large for her frame, and slowly, tentatively, she reached out a hand, hovering it just above the cool metal of the duplicate.

The faint scent of ozone and soldering flux hung heavy in the air. She leaned back, the cheap plastic chair groaning in protest, and stared at the schematic spread across the workbench. Mark kept wiping his brow with the back of his hand, his movements jerky and uncoordinated. The glow of the monitor reflected in his tired eyes, illuminating the identical lines of code blinking on screen. He felt a sudden, dizzying lurch in his chest, a feeling like being off balance on a high wire. He pushed himself up from the stool, his legs feeling unsteady. For weeks, Anya had been wrestling with the final calibration sequence, the frustration manifesting as a constant, low-grade tension in her jaw. She reread the initial design notes, searching for a misplaced variable, anything to explain the uncanny feeling that she wasn't alone in this meticulous, lonely endeavor. When she finally looked up, her eyes snagged on the workbench across the room.
